page.title: multiple sclerosis — Disease Intelligence | OSMF
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for multiple sclerosis: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for multiple sclerosis: 123 alterations and 371 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

gmi_articles.title: The benefits of photobiomodulation in animal models of multiple sclerosis.
Association of exposure to particulate matters and multiple sclerosis.
Cannabinoids may have possible therapeutic properties for the management of the multiple sclerosis.
Dental amalgams (mercury exposure) increase the risk of multiple sclerosis.
Latitude is significantly associated with the prevalence of multiple sclerosis.
Low maternal exposure to ultraviolet radiation in pregnancy is associated with increased risk of multiple sclerosis in offspring.
Mind-body medicine for multiple sclerosis: a systematic review.
Pilates is a feasible therapy for people with multiple sclerosis that can lead to improvements on their physical function.
There is a relation between Epstein-Barr virus and multiple sclerosis.
There is a strong association between UV radiation (sunlight exposure) and multiple sclerosis.
These findings suggest that vitamin D supplementation may have a therapeutic role in the treatment of multiple sclerosis.
Whole plant cannabis provides subjective symptom relief in multiple sclerosis.
High-dose vitamin B supplementation for persistent visual deficit in multiple sclerosis.
The effect of aromatherapy with lavender essential oil on the working memory of women with multiple sclerosis
"Yellow fever vaccination and increased relapse rate in travelers with multiple sclerosis."
A 3-week exercise program on a vibration plate significantly improved muscle strength in persons with multiple sclerosis.
A Mediterranean diet rich in olive oil may have a protective role in multiple sclerosis.
A modified Mediterranean dietary intervention reduced fatigue in multiple sclerosis patients.
Acupressure might be an effective method to reduce fatigue in patients with MS.
Ai-Chi aquatic exercise improves the condition of patients with multiple sclerosis.
Ai-Chi may improve balance, functional mobility, upper and lower extremity muscle strength and fatigue in patients with Multiple sclerosis.
Air pollution by particulate matter PM10 may trigger multiple sclerosis relapses.
Antibody titers against EBV in MS patients rise after the onset of the disease and indicate that vitamin D3 supplementation could limit augmentation of these titers in MS patients.
Antioxidative effects of silymarin on the reduction of liver complications of fingolimod in patients with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis.
Aquatic exercising may improve sexual function in females with multiple sclerosis.
Biofeedback improves bowel symptoms in patients with multiple sclerosis.
Breathing exercises may be beneficial in patients with mild to moderate stages of multiple sclerosis.
Candida infection is more common in multiple sclerosis patients.
Cannabis extracts can be a useful and safe option for patients with MS with moderate to severe spasticity resistant to common antispastic drugs.
Cannabis-based extracts have therapeutic value for bladder dysfunction in advanced mutiple sclerosis.
Cannabis-based medicine is effective in reducing pain and sleep disturbance in patients with multiple sclerosis related central neuropathic pain and is mostly well tolerated.
Cannabis-based medicine may represent a useful new agent for treatment of the symptomatic relief of spasticity in MS.
Cesarean delivery appears to increase the risk of multiple sclerosis.
Citicoline: A candidate for adjunct treatment of multiple sclerosis.
Clinically apparent chickenpox is associated with a lower risk of childhood-onset multiple sclerosis.
Coenzyme Q10 supplementation reduces peripheral oxidative stress and inflammation in interferon-β1a-treated multiple sclerosis.
Continuous fingolimod treatment has been linked to nasopharyngitis (34%), headache (30%), fatigue (19%) and influenza (18%).
Converging evidence from different measures underlines the beneficial effect of sun exposure on MS risk.
CoQ10 supplementation at a dosage of 500 mg appears to decrease inflammatory markers in patients with MS.
Craniosacral therapy is effective for treating lower urinary tract symptoms and improving quality of life in multiple sclerosis patients.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Untreated relapsing MS: ~1 relapse/year; disability accumulates over time in ~85% who convert to secondary progressive MS; spontaneous remission from MS is not possible
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: High-efficacy DMTs (natalizumab, alemtuzumab, ocrelizumab, cladribine) achieve 0 clinical relapses in 50-70% at 2 years and >95% MRI lesion suppression; early high-efficacy treatment shows superior outcomes vs platform therapy escalation
remission.gap_size: Large — massive gap between platform DMT (interferons, glatiramer) and high-efficacy DMT outcomes, but practice still favors escalation model
remission.barrier_detail: Treat-to-target with early high-efficacy therapy is superior to the traditional safety-first escalation model (TREAT-MS, DELIVER-MS trials) but most MS neurologists still start with platform therapies; hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) achieves 70-80% no-evidence-of-disease-activity (NEDA) at 5 years but is rarely offered
remission.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved tolebrutinib and fenebrutinib (BTK inhibitors) for primary progressive MS; ocrelizumab approved for PPMS 2017 (first ever treatment); platform therapies (interferons, glatiramer) unchanged since 1993-1996; paradigm shift to high-efficacy first gaining guideline endorsement only now
remission.outdated_treatments: Escalation approach starting with interferon-beta or glatiramer acetate despite evidence that early high-efficacy treatment substantially reduces long-term disability accumulation; repeated IV corticosteroid use for frequent relapses without reassessing disease-modifying therapy adequacy
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Biotin (high-dose, 100-300mg/day) in progressive MS — Phase 3 equivocal but ongoing; vitamin D optimization (strong epidemiological association, RCT evidence modest); intermittent fasting/ketogenic diet for neuroprotection (animal models; Phase 2 underway); exercise as neuroprotective intervention
remission.frontier_signal: BTK inhibitors (tolebrutinib, fenebrutinib, evobrutinib) for progressive MS — target CNS-resident microglia; remyelination strategies (opicinumab, PIPE-307); HSCT (MIST trial showed superiority over mitoxantrone; Phase 3 vs alemtuzumab underway); CAR-T cell therapies in early trials
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
